speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. This time, we're doing something a little different. Instead of walking through one workflow, we're building your long-term reference shelf. This appendix is the page you keep before the workshop, during the workshop, and after the workshop. It's the permanent bookmark.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is the, "I learned a lot, now where do I actually go next?" episode.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And one important administrative note right up front: this resource guide is meant to live in your fork, so it travels with you. It's not just a reading list. It's your portable support system.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's also a listening note built into the source: this episode is a companion to the appendix. You can listen first as a preview, or after reading as reinforcement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And because the audience here includes blind and low-vision learners, let's not skip the navigation help. That's part of the lesson, not extra decoration.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. For screen reader users, the guide is organized into numbered major sections. In browse mode, jumping by heading is efficient, and the appendix explicitly suggests using heading navigation like H, or moving among level-two headings. Many sections use tables, so table navigation is useful too. And links open in the same tab by default, which matters if you want to keep the guide available while opening resources in a new tab.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the accessibility advice is really practical: use headings to move among sections, tables to move among resource lists, and be intentional about tabs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For low-vision users, the guide notes that most tables follow a consistent Resource, URL, and Notes pattern. If magnification causes overlap, widening the browser or zooming out slightly may help. And descriptive link text matters because it's easier to scan than a wall of raw URLs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted users, there's a table of contents and a reminder that GitHub's own keyboard shortcuts overlay is available with the question mark key on GitHub pages.

speaker: ALEX
text: That consistency is a theme of the whole appendix: same structure, predictable layout, easier navigation.

speaker: ALEX
text: The table of contents itself is worth understanding. It points to sixteen broad categories: the central project, accessibility guides, GitHub Skills, screen readers, VS Code, Copilot, agentic workflows, spec-driven development, command-line and desktop tools, mobile apps, power features, finding contributions, accessibility standards, keyboard shortcuts, community support, and your offline workshop documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone thinks, "I only care about VS Code right now," they can jump there. If someone else thinks, "I need a screen reader setup reference," same story.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the center of the workshop universe: Accessibility Agents. This is the project you forked, contributed to, and now take home.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What resources around that project are considered essential?

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix identifies the upstream repository as the main project, the place your contributions ultimately go. Then it points to a Getting Started guide for your first hour, a full reference guide for the agents and commands, a setup guide for configuration and preferences, a contributing guide for submitting improvements, a security policy for responsible disclosure, and the MIT license, which reminds you the project is meant to be forked, used, adapted, and learned from.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that combination. It's not just, "Here's the repo." It's, "Here's how to begin, customize, contribute, and act responsibly."

speaker: ALEX
text: That's exactly the intent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's the personal fork piece.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. After the workshop, your fork lives under your own GitHub account at a URL like github dot com slash your username slash accessibility-agents. That matters because your fork becomes your practice environment, your reference copy, and potentially your personal working space for future improvements.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you get back into it quickly from VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix gives a fast path: clone your fork with git clone using your repository URL, change into the directory, open it in VS Code with code dot, open Copilot Chat either with the keyboard shortcut or through the Command Palette if your keymap differs, and then try the daily briefing prompt.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a nice detail: it acknowledges shortcuts can vary.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, which is especially important for accessibility setups and custom keymaps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And personalizing the fork means more than renaming it in your head.

speaker: ALEX
text: Much more. The guide says to copy preferences dot example dot md to preferences dot md inside the hidden agents folder, then add your GitHub username, the repositories you use most, and your preferred output format. Commit that file, and now the agents can tailor their responses to your identity and workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So personalization here means making the tools context-aware.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next are the official GitHub accessibility guides. These are especially important because the appendix says they were primary research sources for the workshop documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which means if learners want the most authoritative follow-up, this is where they should go.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The guide lists screen reader documentation for repositories, issues, pull requests, and GitHub Copilot in VS Code. It also includes a guide for custom instructions, a guide for getting started with custom agents for accessibility, and GitHub's broader accessibility settings overview.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we translate that into plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. If you need to navigate repository pages, file trees, or branches with a screen reader, use the repositories guide. If you need help filing, reading, or commenting on issues, use the issues guide. For reading diffs, reviewing changes, and merging, use the pull requests guide. If you're using Copilot in VS Code with NVDA, JAWS, or VoiceOver, there's a guide focused on exactly that. If you want to shape how Copilot behaves, the custom instructions guide helps. If you want to understand what custom agents are and how they differ from instructions, the getting-started-with-agents guide helps. And if you want to tune hovercards, reduce motion, or work with color modes, the accessibility settings overview is the right stop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this section is less about one tool and more about official maps for accessible usage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then we move to GitHub Skills, which is GitHub's free self-paced learning platform.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the appendix makes a pretty strong point about how it works: no separate learning website, no separate login, and no video dependency.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Everything happens inside GitHub itself. The teaching model is a template-copy pattern. You start a course, use the course repository as a template, create a copy in your own account, and then Mona, GitHub's education bot, teaches through issues and pull requests.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk that through slowly because it's an unusual structure if you've only used normal online courses.

speaker: ALEX
text: Step one: you copy the course by choosing Start course, then Use this template, then Create a new repository. Step two: a GitHub Actions workflow runs automatically. In about twenty seconds, Mona opens your first lesson as an issue in your new repository. Step three: you read the issue and perform the task in that same repository. Maybe you make a commit, open a pull request, or resolve a conflict. Step four: another workflow checks your work. If it's correct, you advance. If not, you get feedback. Step five: the cycle repeats until the course finishes and the final issue closes with a success message.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the repository itself becomes the classroom, Mona becomes the coach, and GitHub Actions becomes the auto-grader.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a perfect summary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also very specific screen reader navigation guidance for taking a GitHub Skills course.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. To start, navigate to the module page, move to the Start course button, then Use this template, then Create a new repository. Fill in the repository name and create it. After that, lessons usually live in the Issues tab. The guide suggests using GitHub's navigation shortcuts to reach Issues, then heading navigation to find the current step issue, opening it, reading the instructions in browse mode, doing the task, waiting for Mona's follow-up, and then navigating to comments to read the feedback.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That matters because many learning platforms assume visual scanning. Here, the appendix is saying, no, you can navigate this course structure intentionally with a screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The workflow is compatible with the accessibility skills you already built during the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after the workshop, there's a learning path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix emphasizes that GitHub Skills is free and always available, then recommends a sequence of modules after the workshop. Even though the excerpt we have only shows part of the first item, the core idea is clear: continue in a deliberate order so you reinforce branches, commits, pull requests, and the broader GitHub flow through practice, not passive reading.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next major resource area is screen reader downloads and documentation. This is an important section because it blends setup references with quick command reminders.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And it explicitly includes learning-card style help for setup, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix includes a learning-card section for screen reader downloads and setup, then it breaks out references for NVDA on Windows, JAWS on Windows, VoiceOver on macOS and iOS, and an additional reference section for other screen readers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should learners take from that structurally?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two things. First, the appendix isn't assuming one assistive technology. Second, it recognizes that successful GitHub learning depends not just on GitHub knowledge, but on confidence with your screen reader itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And each of the main screen readers has a quick key-command reference.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. So this isn't just, "Download the software." It's also, "Keep the essential commands near you." For NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver, the quick references are there to support efficient navigation while working in browsers, VS Code, and GitHub interfaces.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the section also points learners outward to standards-oriented references, like WCAG, WAI tutorials, and ARIA practices, in the broader source map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, which helps connect personal tool use to broader accessibility principles.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next up: VS Code resources.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This one is probably deceptively important because a lot of the workshop happens at the intersection of GitHub and VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The appendix includes VS Code resources and specifically calls out a quick way to install the GitHub Pull Requests extension.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why that extension in particular?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because it helps bring GitHub pull request workflows into the editor. For many contributors, especially those using keyboard and assistive technology, reducing context switching between browser and editor can simplify reviewing, discussing, and understanding code changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even though the appendix is a list of resources, the teaching point is: your environment matters. If the editor can surface GitHub context accessibly, that can reduce friction.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then there are GitHub Copilot resources.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I want to be careful here. This section isn't saying, "Use AI instead of learning." It's saying, "Here are the references for using Copilot effectively and accessibly."

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix points learners to Copilot documentation, support information for custom instructions, and material about custom agents. The overall message is that Copilot can be shaped. You can configure it, guide it, and connect it to your own workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which ties nicely into the next section: agentic workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The appendix includes a section on GitHub agentic workflows and specifically a subtopic on how they connect to what you learned in the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the connection?

speaker: ALEX
text: The workshop has already introduced structured prompting, repository context, and accessible contribution practices. Agentic workflows build on that by turning repeated reasoning or task patterns into more systematic flows. Instead of asking for one isolated suggestion, you define roles, expectations, context, and outputs more clearly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's a progression from one-off assistance to repeatable collaboration with tools.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: That progression continues in the Spec-Driven Development section, centered on Spec Kit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is a concept some learners may not have seen before. What does the appendix want them to know?

speaker: ALEX
text: That specs can guide implementation. Rather than jumping directly to code, you begin with a clear description of behavior, expectations, and scope. The section includes a quick start and slash commands, which suggests a practical toolkit for turning structured specifications into actionable workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So slash commands here are shortcuts for invoking well-defined behaviors in a spec-oriented process.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The larger lesson is that good collaboration becomes easier when requirements are explicit and reusable.

speaker: ALEX
text: After that, the appendix turns to GitHub CLI, GitHub Desktop, and Copilot in the CLI.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is one of those sections where learners might realize, "Oh, GitHub is not just the website."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub has multiple interfaces. The appendix includes GitHub CLI, often called gh, with installation guidance and a set of useful commands for contributors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: We don't have the exact command list in the excerpt, but we can still teach the purpose.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The key idea is that gh lets you work with repositories, issues, pull requests, and authentication from the terminal. For contributors who are comfortable in command-line environments, it can be faster and in some cases more accessible than navigating many layers of web UI.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's gh Copilot.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix includes Copilot in the CLI, how to install the extension, how to ask Copilot to explain a command, and how to ask it to suggest a command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a really interesting accessibility support pattern. If you can't remember shell syntax, you can ask for an explanation or suggestion in plain language.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It lowers the memory burden without replacing understanding. You still need judgment, but you don't have to memorize every flag.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Desktop is also included.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That offers another pathway, especially for learners who prefer a desktop application over the command line. The larger teaching point is not that one interface is universally best, but that you should know your options and choose the one that fits your accessibility needs and working style.

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix also includes GitHub Mobile apps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which sometimes surprises people. They think mobile is just for notifications.

speaker: ALEX
text: But it can be more than that. The section covers what you can do in GitHub Mobile and its accessibility features. So learners should think of mobile as a companion interface: triage notifications, read issues, review activity, possibly comment or monitor work while away from a desktop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And accessibility features matter here because mobile platforms often provide strong built-in support like system zoom, screen readers, and gesture-based navigation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The lesson is that contribution workflows can extend beyond a single device.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now we come to a very rich section: GitHub best practices and power features.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the toolbox section.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and it's full of practical, often underused features.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's take them one at a time. Saved replies.

speaker: ALEX
text: Saved replies let you store commonly used messages so you can reuse them in issues and pull requests. The appendix includes both how to set them up and how to use them. This is useful when you repeatedly write similar contributor guidance, review notes, or welcome messages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it saves time and also improves consistency.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Pinned issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: Pinned issues help keep especially important issues visible in a repository. The appendix includes how to pin one. This is useful for onboarding notes, community priorities, contribution guides, or a current call for help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And pinned comments, which the source marks as new in February 2026.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Pinned comments let you keep one especially important comment prominent inside a conversation. The appendix includes how to pin a comment. That can be valuable when a long thread develops and one answer, decision, or instruction should remain easy to find.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That seems particularly helpful for accessibility too, because it reduces hunting through lengthy threads.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Linking to specific lines of code.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is a crucial collaboration feature. The appendix covers the syntax and how to create these links. The purpose is precision. Instead of saying, "There's a problem somewhere in that file," you link directly to the relevant line or line range.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And permalinks are the stable version of that idea.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A permalink points to a specific revision so the link won't drift as the file changes later. The appendix includes how to create one. If you're discussing a historical version of code or documenting an example, a permalink prevents confusion.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Converting issues to discussions is next.

speaker: ALEX
text: And the appendix includes both when to convert and how. The key distinction is whether something is actionable work or open-ended conversation. If a thread is really brainstorming, Q and A, or community discussion rather than a concrete task, discussion space may be more appropriate than an issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That helps keep issue trackers focused.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Team mentions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Team mentions notify an entire group at once. The appendix includes why this matters. In collaborative repositories, that can be the right way to reach maintainers, accessibility reviewers, docs teams, or other specialized groups without guessing at one individual.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there are collapsible sections in Markdown.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix covers the syntax and what to use them for. Collapsible sections can hide optional detail behind a summary label, which is useful when you want a document to be complete without becoming visually overwhelming.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Although as always, authors should think carefully about usability for screen reader users.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. They can be useful, but they should be used intentionally and clearly labeled.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Co-authored commits.

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix includes the syntax and why it matters. A co-authored commit credits more than one contributor in the commit history. This is important for pair work, mentoring, collaborative drafting, and giving visible recognition to everyone involved.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's a deceptively basic topic: watch, star, and fork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and people mix these up all the time. Starring is a lightweight bookmark or endorsement. Watching is about notifications, and the appendix specifically includes watch settings. Forking creates your own copy for experimentation or contribution. Each one serves a different purpose.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then CODEOWNERS, which is powerful and very practical.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. The appendix includes CODEOWNERS syntax, examples, how it works, and what to use it for. The core idea is automatic reviewer assignment based on file paths or patterns.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give the examples the appendix highlights?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It includes patterns like a documentation team reviewing all docs changes, an accessibility specialist reviewing UI changes, a security team reviewing authentication code, and a default owner for everything else.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's a routing system.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. When set up well, CODEOWNERS helps get the right eyes on the right changes quickly and consistently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Sponsors is in this power-features section too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The appendix explains how it works, why someone might sponsor, and how to sponsor. The big lesson is that open source sustainability matters. If a project or maintainer helps you, sponsorship can be one way to support the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The section closes with advanced Markdown features.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The appendix names task lists, aligned tables, Mermaid diagrams, LaTeX-style math expressions, footnotes, and alert or callout blocks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why include these in a GitHub learning resource appendix?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because effective contribution is often documentation work. These features help you communicate status, structure information clearly, show diagrams, cite notes, or draw attention to important warnings or tips.

speaker: ALEX
text: Next, finding more contributions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is the section for the learner who says, "Okay, I can contribute now. How do I actually find work?"

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The appendix includes GitHub search queries to bookmark. The underlying idea is that contribution discovery can be intentional. Instead of wandering randomly, you can use focused searches to find issues, topics, labels, or repositories that match your interests and experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is about building a repeatable search habit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then the appendix gathers accessibility standards and references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is a nice reminder that accessible contribution is not just about using tools accessibly. It's also about understanding the standards behind the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This section includes accessibility testing tools and interactive Git learning, while the broader source map points to WCAG, WAI tutorials, and the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learners are being invited to deepen in two directions at once: technical workflow skills and accessibility knowledge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If you want to review or create accessible software, you need both.

speaker: ALEX
text: The keyboard shortcuts section is another high-value area.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Especially for users who want to move faster or reduce navigation overhead.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The appendix specifically highlights the most important shortcuts to memorize. The broader point is not to memorize every shortcut GitHub offers, but to learn the few that save you the most effort, especially those for navigation and moving to common areas like issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's that earlier reminder that pressing question mark on a GitHub page opens the shortcuts overlay.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. So the resource guide is teaching both specific shortcuts and the meta-skill of finding shortcuts when you need them.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's also a section called learning pathways.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I appreciate this because not everyone is learning for the same reason or with the same amount of available time.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's precisely why the appendix breaks pathways down by role and by time available. In other words, you can choose your next steps based on who you are becoming, maybe contributor, maintainer, documentation writer, accessibility advocate, or based on the time you realistically have, like a short weekly practice window versus a deeper ongoing study plan.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this section is permission to learn strategically, not all at once.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then we reach community and support.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is another administrative section that really should not be skipped.

speaker: ALEX
text: Agreed. The appendix names GitHub Accessibility, the Accessibility Agents community, and the broader open source accessibility community. That tells learners something important: you are not expected to solve every problem alone.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if you need product accessibility guidance, project-specific support, or community wisdom, there are places to turn.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Technical learning becomes much more sustainable when you know where to ask questions and where to listen.

speaker: ALEX
text: The final major area is your workshop documentation as an offline reference.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which I love, because good documentation should still help when the network is unreliable or when you simply want a stable local copy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The appendix includes quick navigation for your workshop docs and positions them as an offline reference set. That means the materials you used during the workshop are not temporary. They become part of your continuing toolkit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's a final note about authoritative sources and a section-level source map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That matters for trust and maintenance. The appendix isn't pretending to be the final authority on every tool forever. Instead, it points back to the authoritative sources behind each section, such as GitHub Docs, the GitHub Changelog, Copilot docs, accessibility standards from W3C, and related references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if something changes, learners know where to verify the latest version.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That source map teaches good habits: use curated notes for orientation, but return to primary sources for updates.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I had to boil this whole appendix down, I'd say it's trying to answer four questions. Where do I go back to? What should I learn next? Which tools fit my workflow? And who can help me?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's excellent. And I'd add one more: how do I keep accessibility central while doing all of that?

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the resource guide is not just a pile of links.

speaker: ALEX
text: No. It's a structured map of an accessible open source practice: your project, your fork, official accessibility guidance, hands-on learning modules, assistive technology references, development tools, AI-supported workflows, contribution discovery, standards, shortcuts, community, and offline documentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And maybe the simplest action item is this: bookmark the page in your fork, keep it close, and return to it whenever you hit friction.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not need to memorize the whole map. You need to know the map exists, know how it's organized, and trust that you can come back to it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a reassuring place to end.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. Keep your reference shelf nearby, keep practicing in your fork, and keep contributing accessibly.
